title: Interior Design Services

narrative.executive_view: Interior Design Services is a selective, pressured AI opportunity rather than a clean automation roll-up. The code includes planning/design as well as construction-contract administration, specifications, and furnishings-related resale [S1]. BLS likewise describes a workflow spanning client discovery, layouts, material specification, ordering, installation oversight, contractor coordination, and site visits [S2]. AI can reduce substantial visualization, documentation, proposal, marketing, and administration effort, but the supplied low labor share (0.2359) means that labor savings apply to only part of receipts: physical goods and procurement flow through the business. The practical investment question is whether a target has a transferable commercial client base, procurement system, and senior team, rather than a founder’s residential taste and contacts. I assess reasonable but not exceptional value creation, moderate retention, an already-active adoption curve, and PRESSURED—not melting—terminal demand.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI exposure is concentrated in creating and revising plans, mood boards, renderings, presentations, marketing, schedules, basic specifications, and office workflows. BLS confirms that designers use CAD for most drawings and BIM for three-dimensional visualization [S2]. Houzz’s 2025 survey of US design-only firms reports that AI is used primarily for document formatting, scheduling, and content generation, while complex client management is less adopted [S3]. That maps to partial, rather than complete, replacement of interior designers: a designer still translates a client brief, checks codes and dimensions, specifies real products, resolves substitutions, coordinates contractors, and visits the site. Graphic-design and administrative roles carry higher task fractions; physical movers and receiving remain low. The four-digit 5414 mix is contaminated by graphic-design activity, so the higher fraction assigned to those supplied roles is a task judgment, not a claim that graphics is proportionally central to a pure interior-design firm.
narrative.value_capture: Whether the firm keeps AI savings depends on its revenue model. Trade reporting describes firms using flat fees, hourly work, cost-plus procurement, and product markups, with no standard fee structure [S4]. For a well-scoped flat-fee design phase, faster production can be retained. For hourly work, fewer hours mechanically reduce revenue; for product-markup and procurement fees, AI drawing speed has limited direct effect while direct-to-consumer shopping can weaken the furniture-margin base. BLS’s task list shows enduring relationship, procurement, and coordination work [S2], which supports a real service moat, but not necessarily a firm-level moat when the principal owns the client relationship. I therefore give a midrange distribution share and moat: the score does not treat the physical work floor as captured value, and it does not use adoption timing to inflate capture.
narrative.adoption_timing: I estimate 32% current effective AI adoption and 2.5 years to 50% effective adoption in the relevant target population. Houzz found nearly one-third of design businesses already using AI, rising to 50% among firms with at least 10 employees [S3]. The frozen target pool begins at the 20–99 employee class, so its firms are plausibly ahead of the all-design average, but the survey measures any everyday business use, not automation of fee-earning design work. Houzz also reports that designers use AI first in routine administration and content, with complex client management lagging [S3]. The selected timing therefore recognizes meaningful current use without equating a chatbot or document tool with full design-process adoption. It is independent of owner age, target count, or M&A evidence.
narrative.consolidation_economics: This code is fragmented, but the frozen dataset identifies 313 firms in the target EBITDA band, a much more usable starting pool than the many sole practitioners below it. ASID’s 2025 succession research covered 63 architecture/interiors firms under 40 employees and found formal succession plans at only 30% of 25–40 employee firms, 12% of 2–10 employee firms, and under 5% of sole practitioners [S6]. That documents a real transition problem, though it does not give an owner-age rate or a buyer-ready target count. There is at least strategic acquisition activity: Chambers acquired C2 Limited, an interiors/planning firm, while retaining its brand and leadership [S7]. This is not evidence of a broad PE roll-up. A 2025 design-and-build transaction was appraised at 3.2x trailing EBITDA [S8], but it had commercial repeat revenue, approved-vendor status, and remodeling exposure, so it is an optimistic hybrid comp. I use that as a restrained purchase anchor and underwrite a modestly lower exit multiple because acquisition success depends on retaining creative leadership and client relationships.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not MELTING. The formal product taxonomy distinguishes full-service design, which includes construction administration and construction documents, from design-only and consulting work [S1]. It also identifies furnishings resale margins as a related product [S1], explaining why labor cost is a low share of receipts and why AI-generated images alone do not erase the business. BLS’s workflow includes product orders, installation oversight, contractor coordination, code awareness, and site follow-up [S2]; those are paid physical-world and accountability functions. Yet client-accessible visual ideation, online product discovery, and low-friction AI content can commoditize the early design phase and erode product-markup transparency. Demand should survive for well-positioned full-service commercial, hospitality, healthcare, and high-touch residential firms, but their mix is unknown and design-fee-only firms face more disintermediation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Evidence is useful but mismatched to the exact acquisition target. Houzz’s sample is US design-only professionals registered on its platform and includes residential and commercial design; it is not a census of 541410 firms or a measure of task-level replacement [S3]. The supplied role mix is the broader 5414 group, so graphic-design wages likely overstate their share in the code. Business of Home articles illustrate practitioner pricing but are not a representative revenue-mix survey [S4]. The specific 3.2x EBITDA transaction is one design-and-build business with favorable commercial characteristics [S8], whereas a broker’s 4–6x general range is marketing guidance rather than disclosed comparables [S9]. Finally, 541410 bundles residential, commercial, contract-administration, decorating, procurement, and resale activity; value capture and terminal demand differ sharply across those lines. Low confidence is therefore intentional, and a real diligence case should reconstruct service fees, product margins, founder-originated revenue, client concentration, trade-account economics, and employee retention before assigning a multiple.

sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54141: Interior Design Services
sources.what_it_supports: Defines interior-design services and separately describes full-service contract administration, design-only services, consulting, decorating, and furnishings resale by establishments classified in 541410.

sources.title: Interior Designers — Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.what_it_supports: Describes interior-design tasks including CAD/BIM visualizations, plans, material selection, ordering, installation oversight, contractor coordination, site visits, and varied state licensing requirements.

sources.title: 2025 U.S. Houzz State of AI in Construction & Design
sources.what_it_supports: US survey of design-only residential/commercial firms: nearly one-third use AI, 50% of firms with 10+ employees use it, and current applications are concentrated in routine administration/content rather than complex client management.

sources.title: How did you land on your fee and markup strategies?
sources.what_it_supports: Trade-practice examples of flat design fees and fee-plus-markup models, published in collaboration with the 2024 Interior Design Business Survey partners.

sources.title: ASID Releases 2025 Economic Outlook Report
sources.what_it_supports: ASID’s current demand context: an uneven outlook affected by high interest rates, material costs, tariffs, labor constraints, and opportunities in adaptive reuse and senior living.

sources.title: New Research Explores Succession Planning For Interior Design Firms
sources.what_it_supports: ASID/Greenway research on 63 architecture and interior design firms under 40 employees, including low formal succession-plan rates and a leadership gap.

sources.title: Chambers Acquires C2 Limited Design Associates
sources.what_it_supports: A verified 2026 strategic acquisition of a planning and interior-design firm by a firm providing planning, architecture, interior design, and procurement services.

sources.title: Firm Announces Deal Closing For An Interior Design & Build Services Company
sources.what_it_supports: Reports a 2025 NYC-metro commercial interior design-and-build company appraised at 3.2x trailing EBITDA, with repeat revenue, approved-vendor status, and other favorable attributes.

sources.title: Selling an Interior Design Business — Valuation Insights
sources.what_it_supports: Broker-published directional interior-design EBITDA range of 4x–6x and stated importance of firm size, client portfolio, reputation, and service diversity.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541410 ($4,053,951,989) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541410 ($18,324,956,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $342,107 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541410); class <5: 13,213 firms, avg revenue/firm $590,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $92,354 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,938 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,036,537 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $318,718 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 673 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,873,851 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $606,258 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 275 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,288,840 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,610,203 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,612,240 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,851,816 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,695,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,368,840 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 313
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: S1 and S2 establish an output that mixes design production with contract administration, procurement, and installation support. S3 directly shows current AI use in routine design-firm operations. Fractions are task judgments for the locked supplied roles: visual/document production and administrative work are more replaceable than client interpretation, site work, contractor coordination, and physical logistics.
caveats: The supplied mix is four-digit 5414 and carries graphic-design/art-direction contamination from sibling code 541430.
The 37.39% residual is a bounded judgment rather than a reweighted six-digit occupational structure.
S3 measures business-tool use among Houzz design respondents, not observed task-level automation of 541410 wage roles.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Plans, visualizations, schedules, and specifications are AI-assisted, while client discovery, code-aware judgment, sourcing, contractor coordination, and site oversight remain human work.
Graphic production, presentation, mood-board, and marketing asset tasks are highly exposed to generative tools, although final brand and client review remain human.
Operations reporting, internal planning, and routine communications are automatable; ownership, staffing, client escalation, and commercial accountability remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead research, outreach drafts, and proposal materials automate, while relationship selling and trust-based conversion do not fully.
Schedules, follow-ups, budget reporting, and routine project records automate; contractor coordination, substitutions, and site exceptions persist.
Document formatting, calendars, billing support, and routine file work are prime current AI applications, while office presence and exception handling remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical receiving, moving, inspection, and handling of furnishings are minimally automated; only notices and paperwork are exposed.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market research summaries, content generation, campaign drafts, and analysis are highly AI-assisted, with positioning and channel judgment retained.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Routine correspondence, meeting summaries, document handling, and administrative tracking are AI-exposed; executive support and sensitive coordination remain.
Concept exploration and presentation production automate materially, but creative direction, client taste translation, and approval retain human responsibility.

The unlisted remainder likely combines junior design/CAD, purchasing, bookkeeping, expediting, and installation-support roles; it is modeled as a balanced mix of automatable information work and physical coordination.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed billing creates mixed retention. Flat-fee scope can retain faster production, hourly fees pass the time saving to the client through fewer billable hours, and procurement markup is less tied to design hours. I select a 40% retained-savings share before moat.
caveats: S4 provides practitioner examples rather than a representative target-size revenue split.
This models pass-through only; it does not claim that product procurement or human coordination is an AI labor saving.

pi_moat.rationale: Full-service work includes construction administration, specifications, product ordering, installation oversight, contractor coordination, and client satisfaction. These create a meaningful relationship and execution moat, but it is weakened when the principal’s personal taste and network originate the revenue.
caveats: Sources establish the service bundle, not a measured firm-level retention or pricing-moat rate.
Licensing varies by state; this estimate does not use regulation as a blanket moat.
Principal portability is a separate deal-level risk that may lower the value for residential boutiques.

t50_years.rationale: Nearly one-third of design firms already use AI and 50% of firms with 10+ employees report use. Because the relevant $1–10M EBITDA targets are larger than the median respondent but complex client tasks lag, I estimate 2.5 years to 50% effective workflow adoption.
caveats: S3 measures any AI tool use among Houzz design-firm respondents, not a 541410 target-band measure of effective task adoption.
This timing does not use succession, market size, transaction activity, or the expected terminal-demand class.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Houzz reports nearly one-third of design businesses use AI. I map that directional result to 32% current effective use, below the 50% boundary because the report says the sector is early-stage and use is concentrated in routine tasks.
caveats: The numeric mapping is a judgment from a qualitative ‘nearly one-third’ statement and the survey is not restricted to NAICS 541410.
The target pool may have higher basic tool use, but that does not necessarily imply higher effective use in client-facing design work.

historical_analogs.value: The current analog is progressive workflow digitization rather than a completed displacement event: BLS records CAD/BIM as standard design production, while Houzz shows AI first entering low-risk administration, scheduling, document formatting, and content generation before complex client management.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources support a phased workflow pattern: digitized drawing and visualization plus early AI in routine work. The inference that AI moves from routine work toward integrated production is stated as an analogy, not observed causal history.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: A gradual augmentation path is better supported than sudden end-to-end replacement.
caveats: Neither source is a longitudinal 541410 productivity study or a direct estimate of future task displacement.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: ASID’s succession research explicitly addresses interior-design firm owners nearing retirement but supplies no owner-age distribution. I use 30% as a cautious proxy for the share over 60 rather than inventing a code-specific age statistic.
caveats: S6 studies succession planning in 63 architecture and interior firms under 40 employees, not owner ages, NAICS-only firms, or the $1–10M EBITDA band.
This demographic proxy is used only for buyability, not adoption or capture.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: ASID/Greenway documents low formal succession-plan rates and a leadership gap among the sampled architecture and interior design firms, which is direct documentation of a succession-planning shortage in the relevant professional-services context.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; true for documented planning and leadership gaps, unmeasured for all 541410 firms
caveats: The study is limited to 63 firms under 40 employees and combines architecture with interior design; it does not measure seller willingness or target-quality supply.

active_consolidators.rationale: Chambers is one verified strategic acquirer of an interior-design studio. I count one verified active strategic buyer rather than asserting a broad roll-up wave or a complete market census.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 0–3 verified strategic acquirers in the visible public evidence
caveats: S7 proves one acquisition in private-club planning/interiors; it does not establish a PE platform, a national consolidator count, or buyer interest in ordinary residential studios.

buy_mult.rationale: A 2025 interior design-and-build transaction was appraised at 3.2x trailing EBITDA [S8]. I use 3.2x as an estimated entry anchor, below the broker-published 4x–6x directional range [S9], because typical targets may have founder dependence and less favorable repeat revenue than the disclosed hybrid commercial example.
caveats: S8 is one NYC-metro commercial design-and-build deal with repeat revenue, approved-vendor status, and remodeling exposure; it is not a standalone 541410 comp set.
S9 is broker marketing guidance, not a disclosed transaction database.
The frozen dataset’s receipt-based margin estimate includes product pass-through and should not substitute for target-level normalized EBITDA.

exit_mult.rationale: I estimate a slightly lower exit multiple than entry because a future buyer will underwrite more visible AI pressure on design fees and the same founder/client portability risk. The observed 3.2x hybrid transaction and 4x–6x broker range are context, not evidence of this future exit.
caveats: This is a forward underwriting estimate, not an observed forecast or a claimed multiple-expansion thesis.
Service mix, client concentration, procurement margins, and retention of creative principals can move the actual exit materially.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed flat design fees, hourly billing, cost-plus/procurement management fees, and product markups; full-service firms may also bill for contract administration and furnishings resale.
pricing_structure.rationale: S1 formally distinguishes full-service, design-only, consulting, and resale products. S4 supplies current practitioner examples of flat fees and fee-plus-markup structures. Together they support a mixed, non-standard pricing description.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Revenue mix varies materially by residential versus commercial work and by whether furnishings flow through the firm.
caveats: S4 is not a representative target-band revenue survey, and S1 is a product taxonomy rather than a financial dataset.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. Task replaceability is based on design-production and administrative work. The 2.5-year adoption estimate is supported separately by current design-firm AI use and the fact that complex client-management use lags routine adoption [S3]; it does not borrow from owner succession or M&A evidence.

terminal_value.justification: The code includes full-service administration, procurement, installation-related coordination, and furnishings resale [S1], while BLS describes real product, contractor, code, and site work [S2]. Those channels preserve paid demand, but AI can commoditize design-production artifacts and pressure design fees and product transparency.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The role mix is broad 5414, Houzz is a platform sample of design-only firms, ASID’s succession study combines architecture and interiors, pricing evidence is practitioner-level, and the EBITDA comp is a single design-and-build transaction. Each proxy is identified in the affected caveats.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI’s effect on flat, hourly, and procurement-markup models is assigned to pi_dist. The task score does not assume pricing deflation, and the human physical-work floor is discussed only in terminal demand rather than treated as capture.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied occupational mix is four-digit 5414 Specialized Design Services. Its 15.38% graphic-designer wage share and 2.96% art-director share likely include substantial 541430 graphic-design activity, while the true 541410 interior-designer share is likely higher than the locked 18.41%.
sensitivity: A six-digit interior-design role mix would likely raise core-interior-designer weight and lower graphic-design contamination; the net V direction is uncertain because both roles are substantially AI-exposed but at different fractions.
The finalizer must retain dataset wage weights, so this packet uses only within-role judgments and a disclosed residual estimate.
Capture and terminal-demand conclusions depend more on fees, procurement, and coordination than on the exact role reweighting.

subsegments: Residential full-service design, decorating, furnishings specification, procurement, and installation coordination
Commercial, corporate, hospitality, healthcare, and institutional interiors with contract administration
Design-only, consulting, and visualization work without construction administration
Furnishings, fixtures, and decorating-supplies resale or commission activity
Architecture-firm interior practices and retailer-attached kitchen/bath or decorating services that may sit outside standalone 541410 operators
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Unknown within the $1–10M EBITDA target pool; the scoring treats the code as a blended full-service interior-design and procurement/coordination business rather than assuming residential design-only dominance.
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census’s product taxonomy assigns both full-service construction administration and furnishings resale to establishments classified in 541410 [S1]; BLS confirms varied building specialties and product/installation duties [S2]. No target-band revenue split was located.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10.00 at one year to 2.50 at four years; the source records tool adoption, not full fee-earning workflow replacement.
C changes materially with the target’s mix of hourly fees, flat fees, and procurement/product economics.
C depends on whether the relationship, trade accounts, systems, and senior team belong to the firm rather than the departing principal.
M is sensitive to normalization of product pass-through, target mix, and the lack of a broad standalone-541410 transaction set.

reviewer_note: All score-driving evidence was live-reopened. The packet does not claim a code-specific AI adoption rate, owner-age census, target revenue mix, consolidator census, or transaction database where none was verified. Estimates are deliberately bounded and caveated; actual acquisition underwriting requires target-level service/product gross margin, client-transferability, principal-retention, and project-pipeline evidence.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=2.5, A=4
